step.title: Step 74
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> SPACE -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, UP, SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 225, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 78 actions; a strong score needs about 58 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 46 actions returned the board to a state already seen 46 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 46 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is over now. Let me check what happened - pressing SPACE caused game_over. Let me look at what changed in the board state.
Looking at the images:
- Step 221: Red block visible, blue bar near it
- Step 222: Red block gone, blue bar visible (red was destroyed!)
- Step 223: Red block reappeared (reset?)
- Step 224: Back to original state
So pressing SPACE destroyed the red block but then caused game over. The level needs to be restarted. Let me check the current state and figure out what to do differently.
Wait, looking more carefully at the sequence:
- Step 221 shows the red block and blue bar next to it
- Step 222 shows the red block is gone (destroyed!)
- But then game_over happened
So the issue is that pressing SPACE destroys the block but also ends the game. Maybe I need to destroy all blocks without pressing SPACE, or maybe SPACE is not the right action.
Actually wait - let me re-read. The game says "game_over" but "level_completed: false" and "run_complete: false". So the game over means I failed, not that I won.
Maybe the goal is to destroy the red block without pressing SPACE? Or maybe I need to collect the blocks in a specific order?
Let me look at the current board state to understand what happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state after game over
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    print(f"color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary'][:4]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The board has reset! I can see:
- Two yellow blocks now (cols 12-23 and 28-39 and 44-55) - wait, 3 yellow blocks!
- Two red blocks (rows 16-19, cols 8-19 and rows 24-27, cols 28-39)
- Blue bar at rows 36-39, cols 20-39
- Magenta and dark gray blocks at bottom
Wait, the game over reset the board. But I see TWO red blocks now and THREE yellow blocks. Before there was only one red block and two yellow blocks. So the level has been reset and maybe the game state changed.
Actually, looking more carefully:
- Before (initial state): 2 yellow blocks, 1 red block
- Now after reset: 3 yellow blocks, 2 red blocks
This means the game over didn't just reset - it changed the level somehow. Or maybe the game over state IS the new state and I need to continue from here.
Wait, the result said "game_over: true" but "level_completed: false" and "run_complete: false". This is confusing. Let me check if I can still take actions.
Actually, the valid_actions list still has all actions. Let me check if the game really is over or if it just means I failed that attempt.
Looking at the images again:
- Step 221: red block at middle, blue bar near it
- Step 222: red block gone (destroyed)
- Step 223: red block reappeared at different position (top left)
- Step 224: blue bar moved back down
So pressing SPACE destroyed the red block, but then something went wrong and the game ended. The board now has a different configuration.
Wait, but the result said "game_over: true". Does this mean I can't continue? Let me try taking an action to see if the game is still playable.
Actually, looking at the step count - it went from 217 to 225, so 8 steps were taken (6 actions + 2 for some reason). The game says "game_over" but I'm still on level 2.
Let me try to take an action and see what happens. Maybe "game_over" just means the current attempt failed and I need to try again from the reset state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving to see if game is still playable after game_over
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
